title: Insurance overhaul to fix hospitals
full_text:
Labor has proposed a major shake-up of private health insurance by simplifying the thousands of types of products on the market, slashing waiting times to access pregnancy cover and forcing funds to share the risk pools for maternity and mental health services.
In a consultation paper released on Thursday, the government said the 5000 different private health insurance products on the market were unnecessarily complex and confusing for customers. It proposed removing so-called "plus" products and eliminating or consolidating other product tiers.
Worried about an exodus of Australians from private health because of soaring premiums, insurers want the government to review the four-tiered gold, silver, bronze and basic product system introduced by the Morrison government seven years ago.
More than 14 million Australians have private health insurance, but the industry is concerned that cost-of-living pressures and the government's changes to the rebate for people over 65, which were part of broader measures in the federal budget, will make people flee private health insurance.
Health insurers welcomed the proposed changes, but said they should be implemented only if they did not trigger a rise in premiums, making health insurance unaffordable.
"Any proposal that increases premiums risks making health insurance less affordable for Australian families," said Rachel David, the chief executive of Private Healthcare Australia, which represents health funds.
"If people can no longer afford private health insurance, participation falls, pressure shifts onto the public hospital system, and the sustainability of the entire private healthcare system is undermined."
Catholic Health Australia, which represents 63 not-for-profit hospitals, welcomed the proposals, which it hoped would be implemented next year. The higher annual health insurance premiums kick in on April 1 and are set by Health Minister Mark Butler.
"This is a serious, substantive package that takes on problems patients and hospitals have been raising for years," CHA acting chief executive Katharine Bassett said.
The Australian Financial Review revealed last month that the Albanese government was looking at changes to private health insurance product tiers, maternity and mental health care and hospitals in the home as part of a public consultation process designed to make the hospital sector more viable and stave off ward closures.
In a series of proposals presented to hospitals and insurers on Thursday, the government proposed an increase in default insurance payouts to more than 100 private hospitals in regional and rural Australia. This means health insurers would have to pay a higher rate for members using those hospitals even if they did not have a negotiated contract.
The paper also proposed a minimum benefit paid by insurers for some hospital-in-the-home services such as intravenous infusions, wound care and palliative care.
Measures aimed at bringing in more overseas-trained psychiatrists to address the shortage of skilled labour in the mental health sector, and new insurance risk pools for mental health and maternity, were also proposed.
Labor has shelved a controversial proposal to introduce a benchmark pricing model for private hospital procedures following opposition from the biggest operator, Ramsay Health Care, to what would have been the most significant shake-up to the private health system in decades.
Butler has also said reducing specialists' fees will be a key focus of the government's health policy reform.
Private hospital operators want private health insurers to increase funding, and health funds argue that many operators are inefficient and have failed to keep up with changing clinical needs.
Private maternity and mental health wards have been the hardest hit because of workforce shortages, fewer women having babies, and psychiatrists preferring to treat patients in out-of-hospital settings.
Butler conducted a review of the private hospital system last year, which found cost growth was outstripping revenue growth. However, he has indicated that the government has no appetite to directly bail out private operators.
Healthscope, the country's second-largest operator, fell into receivership last year with debts of $1.6 billion.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Michael Smith | Health
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
